text: The laparoscopic view shows macronodular cirrhosis of the liver. The omentum from the transverse colon is visible, adhered to the gallbladder. Inflammatory flakes are observed in the surgical field.

The surgeon gradually dissects adhesions between the gallbladder and the omentum of the transverse colon. The gallbladder appears thick-walled and distended, prompting aspiration of pus and infected bile using a needle. A tooth clasper is applied at the gallbladder fundus for frontal traction towards the right shoulder, facilitating further dissection of small adhesions. Gauze is used to manage oozing due to inflammation.

The surgeon uses a gauze with a blunt tip to apply pressure, opening up tissue planes without damaging the gallbladder or surrounding structures. Dissection continues at the level of the anterior peritoneum, revealing fibrotic inflammation in the region.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to identify anatomical planes and landmarks, focusing on small adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding structures. Sharp dissection is performed using low watt electrocautery, ensuring the bowel is kept away from the energy application area. Traction is adjusted to safely dissect and divide the fibrotic, flimsy adhesions.

The surgeon encounters thick and fibrotic adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding structures, requiring sharp dissection with monopolar electrocautery due to their resistance to blunt dissection. Gauze is used to stabilize the surgical field and prevent collapse of the pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon applies traction to the tissues while pushing the gallbladder away from the resection area to minimize trauma and reduce bleeding. Pus is visible from an adherent area on the gallbladder, ensuring no duodenal perforation or cystic communication. An iatrogenic injury causes infected bile to leak, revealing its color and consistency. The perforation site is visible, and a traumatic grasper is used below it for further dissection. The cystic duct and CBD are identified, with the cystic duct appearing short and wide.

The surgeon performs a subtotal cholecystectomy, carefully using energy to divide the lower portion of the gallbladder near the neck and cystic duct region, leaving a one-centimeter tissue margin for safety. This margin is used to ligate the cystic duct opening with sutures or an endo loop.

The surgeon carefully dissects parallel to the incision site, lifting the gallbladder from the liver bed while avoiding deeper cuts that could damage the liver or major vessels. The interior of the gallbladder is visibly gangrenous and sloughing off, emphasizing the need to err on the side of caution during division to avoid proximity to the common bile duct (CBD).

The surgeon completes the division of the final attachments and adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding tissues. A vessel, likely the cystic artery or its branch, is identified and secured with a titanium clip before being sharply divided with scissors, resulting in minimal back bleeding. Remaining adhesions between the gallbladder and liver bed are carefully dissected, and a suction cannula is used to confirm the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon observes that the viable part of the gallbladder wall has become gangrenous, with only slough tissue remaining. Due to proximity to major vessels in the porta area, a small portion of the posterior gallbladder wall is left intact. The surgeon uses energy and suction alternately to maintain a dry surgical field, ensuring not to penetrate too deeply into the liver bed.

The surgeon uses energy intermittently to dissect in the correct plane, ensuring minimal gallbladder wall is left behind and avoiding deep penetration into the liver parenchyma. Inferior to the dissection area, a small portion of the posterior gallbladder wall is visible, while above, the liver appears damaged, and the gallbladder has been removed entirely. The remaining tissue is being divided to proceed further.

The surgeon performs sharp and blunt dissection to identify the correct plane for removing the gallbladder, ensuring hemostasis is maintained. The gallbladder appears edematous and discolored due to inflammation. The final attachments to the liver bed are carefully divided, leaving a small portion for traction. A 2-0 Vicryl suture is used to obliterate the cystic duct opening, with careful attention to avoid injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon tightens the knot securely, complementing it with additional throws to ensure stability, similar to an open procedure. A second bite is taken with careful needle direction, positioned approximately 1.5 centimeters from the confluence of the cystic duct and common bile duct. The third and final bite is taken, securing the tissue and completing the knot. The area is sealed off effectively, and the loop and other sutures are cut.

The surgeon uses a suture for traction to identify the area and secure hemostasis. The common bile duct (CBD) is visible, with the confluence of the cystic duct to the CBD just above the section. The final attachments of the gallbladder are meticulously taken down, ensuring careful dissection to avoid injury and excessive bleeding.

The surgeon carefully dissects the final attachment between the gallbladder and the liver bed, completing the cholecystectomy procedure. Hemostasis is achieved with minimal use of energy, and the surgical field shows no significant bleeding. The gallbladder specimen is placed into an endobag for retrieval.

The surgeon places the gallbladder into an indigenously made endobag, ensuring the fundus is positioned towards the distal portion and the neck near the opening for easy retrieval. The bag is sealed using sutures, facilitating its removal. An abdominal drain is placed subhepatically.
